sentences.text: Yeah.
Yeah.
What happened.
And they-- I tell them and explain them, and I talk to these people.
And they tell me our Red Army is the biggest and strongest in the world.
And we're not afraid for nothing.
And this, what you talk to us, it's foolish because we don't believe it.
We don't believe it.
On the market.
There was a big market over there.
You had plenty of Jewish people, in the hundreds.
And I talked to those people.
I talked with the people.
And it doesn't help with nothing.
And I see that--
You see how they were Jewish.
They talk-- they talk Jewish.
And I talk Jewish to those people.
I cannot talk-- I-- I don't know Russian anyway.
So I talk Jewish, and they understand me.
And so I stay couple days.
I sleep on the-- in the park.
There's a park, on the benches I sleep, a couple days.
And I go to the market.
And I talk to people. it doesn't help me, nothing.
And what happened over there later, the history show what did they in [INAUDIBLE]-- in [?
Bavaria, ?] they put on 30,000 people.
They kill them off over there.
And nobody knows what happen because they don't believe it.
Well, in Kiev I stay maybe 10 days, maximum.
Now that I see the situation, it's no-- it's no good.
Because they-- I know they occupied all-- all the-- the whole-- the whole territory was occupied already, to [NON-ENGLISH], where I was-- well, over there to the-- to the mines.
It was all-- all occupied by the-- by the Germans.
I see the end of Kiev being maybe in two, three, or four weeks, it'll be the end of Kiev.
And the end of-- of who knows, maybe Russia, it'll be the end of Russia.
Who knows?
It was a-- a terrible power.
And they wasn't-- they-- they-- they wasn't prepared rightly.
That's all.
This was, I think, in '41.
I think it was in the fall, I think.
It's in the fall, yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Oh, that 10 days, I tried to escape from Kiev.
And I catch up with the train, with some other people.
And I catch up.
And I went to Kharkov.
Kharkov, it's a big city.
Ah, because they-- you cannot go by trains.
You must go and slip in under the train, inside or back, some way to go through.
Because all of the people traveling, you're hiding somewhere, that's all.
This is the way to do this, so hiding.
And I go around, day and night, I'm staying-- hanging someplace.
And I came to Kharkov.
And I came to Kharkov and start another story.
And we must find a place to go, from Kharkov, another places, yeah.
There was all kind of people, what we were--
Yeah.
No, I got some other people, what I know, yeah.
Yeah.
So we went to Kharkov.
And then from Kharkov, start another story.
And from Kharkov, I see the situation.
And in a short while, we find out Kiev is over.
So from Kharkov, we tried to go--
It's came-- some-- some rumors or some people run away.
Some way-- some way, you got the-- the news came into you.
That's-- we know we must go.
So I went to some store and buy a-- in the store where they're selling mops and the-- the school supplies, and they got mops.
And I take a look and the mop [INAUDIBLE] to Israel.
How I can go to Israel, [INAUDIBLE].
[LAUGHS]
So I think well, I'll got to the Persian border.
Maybe I can go over there somewhere.
So I-- from Kiev, I went to a place called Bukhara-- Tashkent.
I went to Tashkent.
And from Tashkent, to Bukhara.
Maybe I can go from over there, or through the borders.
How did you go from one place to another?
Just by trains.
Just by trains--
You know what I-- trains, yeah.
Trains to somewhere, this was all kind of troubles, yeah.
Yeah, Jewish person.
Jewish, yeah.
Jewish.
I feel like a Jew, he must run away from trouble.
That's it.
No, they don't know-- they know something happened to-- this was a period of-- the whole Russia was in turmoil.
It was in turmoil.
It was a war.
And they lost a lot, and they start to organize again, to fight the war.
That's-- So I was like a-- I was without a passport, without nothing, they catch me.
So--
Oh, we eat-- go grab something and go to the bazaar and buy something or change something what you got, a piece of bread or something.
This was-- we survive this-- survivorship, you see?
You know, from day to day, you never know what's coming tomorrow.
Oh yeah, this is-- well, another story.
I was-- I was a prisoner of-- I was a prisoner for time being, in some place.
They catch me and they send me to work, like a-- almost like a prisoner.
They send me to-- it's not far.
It's in between Siberia and the-- and the-- and other places, very north of Russia.
Yeah, they send me over there.
And I was in a place.
They call this [RUSSIAN].
And over there, I working for the-- I start to work like, they call this in Russia, [RUSSIAN] and in English, this would be a cooper, I think.
A cooper, you know what is a cooper?
A cooper is making barrels.
So they put me over there, working.
It's hard work.
I don't know it.
They got-- give me instruments.
I make the barrels over there.
And the barrels, they put on the meat, with salt.
And they send this to the front.
So it was a lot of like, people what's prisoners, working.
They-- this we do.
And some with one eye, some with no legs, or something, some people what-- what they can't do something a little bit.
And then they put me over there.
They give you bread-- piece of bread a day, and a soup.
I stay there for a couple-- I don't know how long.
You know, the war start, it was '42 already, or '43 or '42.
And I was over there.
And I see the situation was so big-- bad, you can starve to death and working hard.
Well, anyway, some day they came to this place.
It was like a big barn.
And they came to the barn.
And they got a list.
And they got every-- everybody's paper was in the-- with these two officer.
One of us over there, and another officer.
And they got a list of these people.
Everybody was staying here, like you stay.
It's nothing, just a piece of a shirt.
And they take us-- everybody out.
Like you stay, everybody out.
This--
A piece of a shirt and a piece of pants, old pants.
Like you stay, everybody out.
Everybody.
And on the-- the-- this-- this-- this-- and come in there with this-- with this.
With the bayonets, with this, they take you out to the-- to the station.
The train station.
Everybody.
We-- we was prisoners, like you have prisoners.
To the station, everybody.
And down we went to the station over there.
Then they take the all people.
And they was, over there on the station, like a doctor, or a lady doctor or something, with somebody.
They stay, and they take a look on they.
They clap you.
They put on the paper, [RUSSIAN].
This means go then, to the front.
Yeah.
But--
The Russian army, that's who.
They need people.
There were people dying over there like flies, everybody.
Like they say to everybody, [RUSSIAN].
And I'm to-- because they don't care, Polack, not Polack, they need people.
And I got the idea, somebody tell us-- well, I know, but I don't know who tell us.
These kind of people, what they got, these-- this-- from the jails and some things like this, they don't-- they send you to the front.
No, they send you up front.
The tanks go back.
And then because there are all these-- there's all these places, it's all with mines.
So this kind of people like we, prisoners, they send them front.
They must go through the mines.
And they go-- can't go back because there any cover they got with the rifles, they kill you anyway.
So we must go in the front, in the line.
And when you go, you fall dead, the tanks can go through.
Because there's a front line.
The tanks go back and can go through the front.
And this-- well, you die, you die.
The-- the mines is open.
Now the front is ready to go.
You know, these kind of people, they don't care about you people anyway.
So I-- I know what will happen to me.
I would go in the first line.
And when I go back, they kill me or the mines kill me.
You know, they must have a front line open.
You know, these kind of people--
It came, a big train.
And this was one train, one line, this they call this [RUSSIAN] Connection.
It's one line go from Vladivostok to Moscow, back and forth.
One line, just a big line.
And this line, it came-- and our station, [INAUDIBLE], it was-- they call [INAUDIBLE], had this-- I mean, the-- the one train came from Vladivostok, from-- from the over there, from the Far East.
And one train go from Moscow, this way.
And on this station, they changed.
They take water and coal and go this way and this way.
Or you go this way to the front or this way to Vladivostok.
So my [INAUDIBLE], I know the know.
I talk to some people.
They tell me they take us this way.
To go this way, to the front.
Because they need people over there they don't care about.
So I think to myself, Gabriel, better get in trouble.
[LAUGHS] Trouble-- down there everybody, they got their papers.
You don't have the paper no more, nothing.
They got the-- these papers.
And they take everybody in the wagons, close up the doors over there.
It was a regular train, a big train in every-- in every compartment, 10 people, how much they put on, they put on.
And I see there's trouble is going.
It was no good.
I tell to myself, why, if I've-- became a [INAUDIBLE] with the blotter.
So the train don't stop at the stop.
And-- and I went to the door.
And I start to knock the door with my hand and feet.
And I start talking in Russian, screaming, [RUSSIAN].
In that's the-- on-- Which it means?
Other-- open the door.
I suppose to go to the-- to the-- to the bathroom.
I got full pants in Russian, I tell him.
I learn Russian.
And I scream and-- and-- and use all kind of words.
And then-- they-- they-- stay with the-- with the stick.
All right.
Then they open the door.
[RUSSIAN].
That mean go to the lavatory.
And you close up the door.
And I keep the pants like this.
I go to the lavatory.
And there was the lavatory in the corner of this.
I go to the lavatory.
And the train start--
--moving.
I close-- when I go to the lavatory, there was a hook over there.
I go in.
I pull down the hook.
Nobody can open the door.
And I see-- I see the window got loose.
I open the window.
And when the train start to go more like 25, 30 miles an hour, I switch over the other side of the window and I hang with my hand.
And this was over there, some-- when the train go like this, it was-- like this, you know?
Like how they call it [NON-ENGLISH].
And it means it's higher.
The train was higher.
And downstairs you got earth and-- and snow.
And no, I mean stones and something like this.
So I was hanging and making the movements with my legs, how the train go out.
When the train go out a little bit and I see, you know, stones, you know, big boulders.
Train go 30 miles an hour.
That's OK.
So a boulder, and then I turn around.
And I fall down like this, fall down, down.
And the train went-- psh-- with my papers, with everything, went to the front over there.
I don't see them no more.
Now I think to myself, God help me.
I tell-- [LAUGHS] I tell him [HEBREW], I know I'm another day.
So start to be darker.
And I go back.
Go back-- back to another train is waiting to go the other way.
Yeah, the other way.
And then I go over there.
And the train was staying, taking in-- taking in water and coal.
And I go in and I-- I catch up someplace over there.
And I go over there.
Night and day, is when I start to go to a place called Turkestan.
And over there I-- I don't go into the town.
Because when-- before we got in the town I jump out.
Because I know the town is always with-- then covered there-- waiting--
--waiting, papers.
Papers.
They kill me.
[LAUGHS] Otherwise I'm a spy, goodbye Charlie.
So I waited for the night.
And I go into the town.
Over there I find some people, Jewish people, living over there.
Some-- some that helped me.
They helped me.
I got another piece of paper.
Yeah, they gave me papers.
They gave me some money.
I got a piece of paper that I'm-- so it's safe.
With all the papers, it was a piece-- they called this [RUSSIAN].
I mean, that meant without nothing.
You can do everything but you end up in jail or something.
They give me some papers that's called-- it's temporary-- temporary papers for living in the city, something like this.
And I got a piece of paper.
So it is good.
So this story ended with this.
And then-- then-- then I was going again, some places.
Do--
I go north, some places.
From this place I go north.
I was-- I was something in--
All kind of-- all kind of people, what they just run away from-- oh.
All kind of--
Not a special person.
Everybody look for him-- for himself in this situation.
Everybody must be from them self because you can't depend on nobody in these kind of situations.
Girls?
No.
Girls was-- I'll tell you about the gross.
Happened to me when I was in some places.
I was in some places like this, like a prisoner in some places.
I can't remember these places and all kind of place.
I-- I go back.
And I went back to the Polish Army because they start to form another Polish Army, Buzuluk.
So I went over there to the Buzuluk.
And they start to form an army, Polish Army because they got a special treaty with the Polish government in London to make a new Polish Army.
And this Polish Army, under General Anders, supposed to fight, again, the Germans.
So I went to the army.
And I was-- I met my-- a friend of mine's.
God forbid too, was over there.
He died.
In this-- in this compound-- in this-- in this whole compound, what that's supposed to make the armies, start a big epidemic of typhus, black typhus.
And I was one of the victims.
The army go away to Persia, to Persia, to Israel.
And I was left around, like me, a lot of people what that--
Yeah.
To Israel.
Army, again, are Anders'.
Yeah, they give them a chance to go out.
This was the end of '42, I think.
This is Polish army.
They're-- because they got a treaty with the Polish government in London.
And Stalin-- Stalin give approval for the Polish army.
And they form the Polish army.
And the army went from this place in Buzuluk.
They went to the border of-- of Persia.
They go through.
And they went to Israel, to Palestine.
Because from Palestine--
No, they're suppose-- it was Polish Army.
It was Jewish, maybe 5% of them, Jews.
They take up-- not everybody.
And Menachem Begin was in the army over there, Menachem Begin.
They--
They approve him.
Yeah, Menachem Begin, they approve him.
He was the surgeon.
They approve him because he get a lot of connections, something I remember.
He was [INAUDIBLE].
I was-- listen, I got sick.
Typhus, it was, over there.
And with typhus, left over-- I was left over and kaput.
They went, the army-- forget it.
And after this, when they went out, the politic change.
The Polish go and make a-- a decision they don't-- that Stalin don't want this kind of agreement.
He break the agreement, and no Polish Army.
And the Russian government start to form a Polish Communistic party.
Now, I was sick at this time.
I was sick.
And when I got this typhus, they went these-- these people, what were sick, no hospitals.
So there's-- this place was calling, I don't know, it's in Bukhara, some other-- I remember, a small place.
And they put all the sick people, all kind of people.
There was no hospitals.
They put on-- everybody on the floors.
On the straw, laying on the floors.
And people mostly dying from this.
I was, over there-- I don't know, about 28, 29, or something like this.
And I was thinking I'd be dying too.
Because I got-- I was very sick.
See, this is a terrible sickness.
The sickness gots the tendency to keep you alive for seven days.
When you go through seven days, or you alive or you died or you got some complications.
So I was laying over there, luckily-- people dying.
And they-- when they die, you lay down.
Every die-- every day, pass by a man with a-- with the-- he got a piece of like-- how do you call this?
A bag with bread, pieces of bread.
And everybody got a piece of bread they put behind their head, here.
So luckily a-- a man is laying around here dead for a couple days.
You know, I take this piece of bread, put it in my mouth.
So I got more bread.
I got more life.
So before they take him out and put down over there, outside in the hole over there, so I got a couple days relief-- Extra ration.
--with bread.
Because I take out the bread.
They put another piece of bread anyway.
So I got a piece of-- well, somebody die from this side, from this side.
And I was alive.
So I take this piece of bread.
I got two pieces of bread.
This mean a lot.
And that's how you saved--
Yeah, saved my life.
So anyway, I lay over there, down.
I lay over there.
And I got these complications.
Everybody after the sickness got complications.
Or the legs or the--
Not the stomach, mostly the urinary trouble.
Kidney or with the stinging, you know, relieves himself on the water.
I mean, dying of-- of [INAUDIBLE] or something like this.
I got complication with my legs.
And the pain was unbelievable.
I was thinking I'm dying every minute.
And I was screaming.
And I just scream and scream.
And I scream in Russian, [RUSSIAN].
It mean cut off my legs and I'll be OK.
And I was screaming.
That's what they can do, screaming.
No medication.
Nothing.
Nothing.
Laying around over there.
That's it.
All of a sudden, some day pass by.
They say-- it was-- they say the doctor pass by.
It was a big lady, tall lady, a nice-looking lady.
And she pass by.
And beside-- behind her was a-- a sister.
You call this--
A nurse, yeah.
Behind her was a nurse.
And she was a tall, nice-looking lady.
And she ask-- she passed by me, stopped me.
And she took a look at me.
And I look at her.
And she ask me why I scream so much?
I tell them I-- to a doctor, I want a doctor, please, [RUSSIAN], mean cut off my legs, I'll be OK.
[LAUGHS] Because I cannot stand, no more, this pain.
I die from the pain.
And I look on her.
She-- and she is start to talk to me.
She tell me, [RUSSIAN].
That mean in Russian, where you came from?
I tell her I'm from Warsaw, Poland.
I am a [INAUDIBLE].
I was in the Polish Army, something like this.
And I look at her.
I see the Jewish eyes.
[LAUGHS] I see she's Jewish.
So then she tell me, yeah, I am from Moscow.
She tell I am Moscow.
I am the chief director of the whole area, and they sent me to check this thing, to check and do something to help.
And they send me here.
And I look at her.
She look at me.
And she tell me something, Russian.
She tell me Russian goodbye.
And she write a piece of paper she give to the-- to this girl.
For the--
To the Nurse So anyway, I got the pain.
I laid in paid.
The next day-- the next day came two people, Uzbeks, with a-- with a wagon, it was a flat wagon with two wheels and a small donkey.
And they take me out.
And they-- and I was thinking they take me out, outside, to the hall.
And the big hall, they put down everything.
Where they putting the-- the white stuff over there.
So I was thinking they take me out and put that white stuff and goodbye.
No, they put me on the -- on the stuff, on this.
And they take me to a couple miles away from this hospital.
Well, hospital, you can call it hospital.
You call it a morgue, mostly, like a hospital.
[LAUGHS] They take me away.
They put me under the seat.
They go and they're on an hour or more.
And there was, over there, a place.
This-- they called it [NON-ENGLISH].
And this was off-- for officers, for they came from the front with injuries, what they're suppose-- came to itself, this.
And they opened the door over there.
It was on the-- like, on the hill.
They open the door.
They take me over there.
And there was big things with water, like this.
They put me in the water, two women, naked.
And with big brushes, they brush off the louses.
I got so many louses.
They brush with those heavy louses.
And one woman cut off the hair with the machine.
And the louses, they brush me off good.
And when they brushed more off they put some water, again, on me.
The-- the all louses go out.
And they take me this-- to a place just like a-- look like a-- a neat hospital.
And there was about a couple peasants in this-- everything, officers, Russian soldiers from the front.
And I was-- she put me over there, in this place like this.
She save my life.
I'd be over there, dead.
She saved my life.
Yeah.
In Russia I was from 1941 to 1945, almost.
The end of '45, yeah.
Yeah, I tell you this-- this in the hospital alone, when this Hospital--
Yeah.
No, no, not my wife.
This-- in this hospital was a special occasion.
I was in this hospital.
It was a lady, and not a lady, a nurse.
And she take care on-- on this-- on this operation for this couple pieces officers.
She don't know I'm officers.
Knows I came here, I must be something.
And-- and she was-- she came from the front too.
She got a bullet.
It went-- went here.
And the hand was a little stiff.
She was a nice-looking girl.
Because I remember her hair, she got her like you know, here she got from both side.
I don't know, she got hair like this, from both side like this, tall.
And she tell me she is from [INAUDIBLE].
And she'll be taking care of me.
And I was looking like not from here, from the dead, half dead.
And she take me to this--
No, no, she's from [RUSSIAN].
[NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH].
She was tall, about-- over six foot, I think this woman-- [LAUGHS] A young woman, about 19 years old.
And she take care of me.
She take me in this corner.
She wash me off.
And-- and she give food.
Every day she bring me a [NON-ENGLISH].
No, she don't know nothing about it.
She don't know I'm Jewish.
I don't look Jewish over there.
I look like a skeleton, mostly.
And she likes me because she-- one time she-- she shave me.
She shave me.
When she shave me, I-- I kiss her hands.
[LAUGHS]
So and she liked me very much.
Every time she give me this [NON-ENGLISH].
And one day she came to me.
She opened here.
And between the breasts, she take off another [NON-ENGLISH].
And she looked around.
Nobody see, she puts it in my mouth.
Two [NON-ENGLISH].
In Russia, it was the [NON-ENGLISH] was made from-- Dough?
Dough, and inside was horse meat.
Because it was sweet.
And she give me all-- every day, a [NON-ENGLISH] from here.
And she-- she-- she steal from the kitchen.
She takes-- she take a big chance.
So I tell her, [?
Marusha, ?] what I can do for you?
She tell me, don't worry.
[LAUGHS]
So I kiss her again, what I can do for her?
I tell her I'm not-- I-- I explain in Russian, I am not a man, you know?
She was-- she was a hot woman.
I can do nothing.
I tell her I am not a man anyway.
You know, she likes me.
How long did you stay there?
About a couple weeks, two or three weeks.
I came to myself, more this [NON-ENGLISH], more-- more life.
Yeah, feel better.
Yeah.
